title: Black olive focaccia
subtitle: A high-hydration slab bread with deep dimples and a shatteringly crisp crust
excerpt: This focaccia uses a 75 percent hydration dough and a generous amount of olive oil to produce a bread with a crunchy, blistered crust and an open, airy crumb. Black olives are pressed into the surface just before baking, where they caramelise slightly in the pockets of oil. The method uses stretch-and-fold rather than kneading.
sourceNotes: Focaccia has roots in ancient Ligurian flatbreads and is documented across northern Italy, particularly Genoa, where the classic version uses only olive oil, salt and rosemary. The olive-topped variant is common throughout Puglia and Sicily. The no-knead, high-hydration method described here draws on techniques popularised by bakers including Jim Lahey and widely published in British food press from 2010 onwards.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut into portions and freeze for up to 2 months. Reheat at 200 degrees C fan for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be mixed and left to bulk-ferment overnight in the fridge, covered. Transfer to the oiled tin the next morning, dimple and proof for 45 minutes before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pressing deep indentations into risen focaccia dough with well-oiled fingers just before baking. The dimples serve two purposes: they collect the drizzled oil, which heats and blisters in the oven to create the characteristic crunchy crust, and they prevent the surface from ballooning unevenly as the dough expands. Press firmly all the way to the base of the tin, not just the surface, and work quickly so the dough does not deflate more than necessary.

content.text: This is a wet, sticky dough that should not be kneaded. The structure comes from three sets of stretch and fold during the bulk ferment, and from the olive oil in the tin, which coats the outside of the dough and prevents it from drying out. Use a rectangular baking tin or a deep roasting tin; the dough needs sides to push against.

Do not add the olives until the final step, just before loading into the oven. Adding them earlier means they sink into the dough during the proof and their brine dilutes the oil pools that form the crust.
comes first, then tuck the olives into the dimples and drizzle generously with oil.

items.prepNote: divided: 20 ml in the dough, 40 ml for the tin and topping

content.text: Combine the 500 g flour, 4 g yeast and 10 g salt. Pour in the 375 ml tepid water and 20 ml olive oil. Mix with a spoon or wet hand until no dry flour remains. The dough will be very wet and shaggy. Cover and leave for 30 minutes.
Bulk ferment with stretch and fold, 2 hours

Perform three sets of stretch and fold at 40-minute intervals during the first 2 hours. Wet your hand, lift one side of the dough up and fold it over the centre. Rotate the bowl and repeat four times in total. The dough should feel noticeably more structured after the third set and pull away from the bowl sides cleanly.

Pour 30 ml olive oil into a 30 x 20 cm baking tin and tilt to coat. Tip the dough in and gently stretch it toward the corners. It will not fill the tin immediately; leave it for 10 minutes and stretch again. Cover and proof for a further 30 minutes until puffed.

Heat the oven to 230 degrees C fan. Oil your fingers and press deep dimples all over the dough, pressing to the base of the tin. Press the drained olives into the dimples. Drizzle the remaining 10 ml olive oil over the surface and scatter with flaky salt. Bake for 22 to 26 minutes until the surface is deep golden brown with darker blistered patches and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before tearing.

items.problem: Focaccia is thick and doughy rather than airy
items.solution: The bulk ferment was cut short or the stretch-and-fold sets were skipped. Both are necessary to build the open structure. A longer, slower ferment in the fridge overnight produces a better result than a quick room-temperature rise.

items.problem: Olives sink to the bottom during baking
items.solution: The olives were added too early. Add them immediately before the loaf goes into the oven, pressing them only partway into the dimples so they sit in the oil pools on the surface.

Not enough oil in the tin, or the oven was not hot enough. The oil should pool visibly around the dough, not just coat the tin. A fully preheated 230 degrees C oven is required for the crust to crisp properly.
